does: This paper quantifies bias in large language models by systematically comparing how LLMs respond to explicit demographic declarations versus implicit sociolinguistic signals like African American Vernacular English and Singlish. Using a factorial experimental design with over 24,000 responses from Gemma-3-12B and Qwen-3-VL-8B across sensitive domains, the authors measure differences in refusal rates and semantic similarity to reference texts. They reveal a counterintuitive finding: users receive better model performance by communicating through dialect markers associated with a demographic group than by explicitly stating membership in that group, suggesting that explicit identity prompts trigger overly aggressive safety filters while implicit linguistic cues evade these mechanisms.
connects: This work builds on growing research examining demographic bias and fairness in large language models, extending prior work that documented performance disparities across racial and socioeconomic groups. It connects to long-standing sociolinguistics research on how language variety correlates with social identity and discrimination, adapting these insights to AI safety contexts. The paper responds to recent work identifying disparities in LLM outputs based on stated user demographics while introducing a novel angle: decoupling explicit identity claims from the implicit linguistic signals through which identity is naturally conveyed. This connects to broader AI safety literature examining how model alignment and safety filters interact with training data biases and the socially-coded nature of language.
enables: This research opens several important directions for improving LLM fairness and safety. It highlights the need to redesign safety mechanisms that currently overfit to explicit demographic markers while remaining insensitive to implicit signals, pointing toward more nuanced alignment approaches that account for sociolinguistic variation. The findings suggest developing evaluation frameworks that test both explicit and implicit identity signals across diverse domains. Future work could investigate why this paradox exists in model training and deployment, potentially leading to safety mechanisms that achieve equitable performance without relying on crude demographic categories. This also enables better understanding of how real-world linguistic diversity should be represented in training data and fine-tuning processes to serve diverse user populations more equitably.
